Triggers.value: People who look similar to the victims

Threat.value: Suddenly feeling or acting as if the stressful experience were actually happening again

Memory.value: When I remember the frightening event I feel like it is happening right now.
When I have memories of what happened I sometimes hear things in my head that I heard during the frightening event.
I remember the frightening event as a few moments, and each moment is a picture in my mind.

Negative Appraisals.value: I feel like I don't know myself anymore
The event happened to me because of the sort of person I am

Maladaptive Strategies.value: I try to push them out of my mind
Situations that put my body under stress

vignette:
He is a 45-year-old South Asian widower who presents with persistent distress following bereavement, with a PCL-5 score of 37 suggesting clinically significant post-traumatic stress symptoms. Since the death, he has become increasingly unsettled by ordinary reminders in daily life. People who look similar to the victims, certain related sounds, and moments that leave him feeling trapped or with no options all seem to jolt him into a state of alarm. In those moments, he can suddenly feel as if the event is happening again, with brief, vivid flashes of the experience intruding into the present.

His recollections are fragmented and sensory, often returning as a few still images rather than a coherent narrative. At times, when he remembers what happened, he feels as though it is happening right now, and he may even hear echoes of what he heard during the frightening event in his mind. These experiences are accompanied by poor sleep, trouble concentrating, and a persistent sense of being on edge. The memories are not simply sad; they are immediate and disruptive, repeatedly drawing him back into the emotional intensity of the loss.

He has developed harsh views of himself in the aftermath, describing that he cannot rely on himself, that he no longer knows who he is, and that the event says something fundamental about the sort of person he is. These beliefs weigh heavily on him and sit alongside his ongoing distress, which seems to intensify both the sense of current threat and the vividness of his memories. He appears caught between the immediate reliving of the bereavement and a deteriorating sense of self.

In coping, he tries to push the thoughts out of his mind, avoids sexual intimacy, and stays away from situations that place his body under physical stress. These efforts may offer brief relief, yet they have narrowed his life and left him more isolated and less engaged with others. The overall picture is one of a man struggling to regain a sense of safety and continuity after loss, with reminders, intrusive memories, and self-doubt maintaining a cycle of alarm and withdrawal.
